---
title: "Interactive Global Health Trend Explorer"
subtitle: "A Live R Shiny + World Bank API Pipeline"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    transition: fade
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    chalkboard: false
    footer: "Global Health Trend Explorer · April 2026"
    css: |
      .reveal h1, .reveal h2 { color: #0D1B2A; }
      .reveal .slide-number { color: #0D9488; }
      .teal { color: #0D9488; font-weight: bold; }
      .navy { color: #0D1B2A; }
      .muted { color: #64748B; font-size: 0.85em; }
      .card {
        background: #F0F4F8;
        border-left: 4px solid #0D9488;
        border-radius: 6px;
        padding: 0.6em 1em;
        margin: 0.4em 0;
      }
      .grid-3 { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr; gap: 1em; }
      .grid-2 { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 1.2em; }
      .stat-box {
        text-align: center;
        background: #0D1B2A;
        color: white;
        border-radius: 8px;
        padding: 0.8em;
      }
      .stat-box .num { font-size: 2em; font-weight: bold; color: #5EEAD4; }
      .stat-box .lbl { font-size: 0.8em; color: #CBD5E1; }
      .pipeline-step {
        background: #0D9488;
        color: white;
        border-radius: 6px;
        padding: 0.5em 0.8em;
        text-align: center;
        font-weight: bold;
      }
      .arrow { font-size: 1.4em; color: #0D9488; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; }
      .code-label {
        background: #0D1B2A;
        color: #5EEAD4;
        font-family: monospace;
        font-size: 0.75em;
        padding: 2px 8px;
        border-radius: 4px;
      }
      .tradeoff-pro { border-left: 4px solid #0D9488; }
      .tradeoff-con { border-left: 4px solid #F97316; }
      .reveal pre { font-size: 0.62em; }
      .img-mockup {
        border: 2px solid #CBD5E1;
        border-radius: 8px;
        padding: 1em;
        background: white;
        font-family: monospace;
        font-size: 0.7em;
        color: #334155;
      }
execute:
  echo: false
---

## {background-color="#0D1B2A"}

::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; justify-content:center; height:85vh; padding-left:2em;"}

<p style="color:#5EEAD4; font-size:0.9em; letter-spacing:0.12em; text-transform:uppercase; margin-bottom:0.2em;">5-Minute Presentation</p>

<h1 style="color:white; font-size:2em; line-height:1.15; margin:0 0 0.3em 0;">
  Interactive Global<br>Health Trend Explorer
</h1>

<p style="color:#CBD5E1; font-size:1.05em; margin:0 0 1.5em 0;">
  Real-time health analytics via R Shiny + World Bank API
</p>

<div class="grid-3" style="max-width:600px;">
  <div class="stat-box"><div class="num">3</div><div class="lbl">Indicators</div></div>
  <div class="stat-box"><div class="num">4</div><div class="lbl">Countries</div></div>
  <div class="stat-box"><div class="num">60+</div><div class="lbl">Years of Data</div></div>
</div>

:::

::: notes
**[Speaker 1 — ~30 sec]**

Hi everyone. We built the Interactive Global Health Trend Explorer — a fully reproducible R Shiny application that pulls live data from the World Bank API and lets you explore health trends interactively, with no setup required.

In five minutes we'll cover what problem it solves, show you the app live, walk through our key design decisions, and tell you what we'd do with more time.
:::

---

## The Problem {background-color="#F0F4F8"}

::: {.grid-3}

::: {.card}
**📊 Fragmented Data**

Global health indicators are spread across dozens of sources with inconsistent formats and update schedules.
:::

::: {.card}
**⏱ Static Snapshots**

Most analyses are frozen in time — no interactivity, no ability to compare years or countries on demand.
:::

::: {.card}
**🔁 Poor Reproducibility**

Manual CSV downloads and ad-hoc scripts make analyses nearly impossible to verify or update.
:::

:::

<br>

::: {style="text-align:center; font-size:1.05em; color:#0D9488; font-style:italic;"}
**We needed one tool: live data, interactive exploration, zero manual steps.**
:::

::: notes
**[Speaker 1 — ~40 sec]**

The problem has three layers.

First, global health data — things like life expectancy or infant mortality — is scattered. The World Bank, WHO, and UN all publish overlapping datasets in different formats.

Second, most tools give you a static chart. You can't drag a slider and instantly see how Brazil's infant mortality changed from 1980 to 2020.

Third, reproducibility. When an analyst downloads a CSV, tweaks it in Excel, and pastes it into R, that workflow is invisible — you can't re-run it six months later.

Our app solves all three by pulling data live from the World Bank's public API on every query, with no manual steps.
:::

---

## Live Demo: What It Does {background-color="#F0F4F8"}

::: {.grid-2}

::: {}
**Controls (Sidebar)**

<div class="img-mockup">
┌─────────────────────────┐<br>
│ Select Country:         │<br>
│  ● United States  ▼     │<br>
│                         │<br>
│ Select Indicator:       │<br>
│  ● Life Expectancy ▼    │<br>
│                         │<br>
│ Year Range:             │<br>
│  [====|—————|====]      │<br>
│  2000              2020 │<br>
└─────────────────────────┘
</div>

<br>

::: {.muted}
All three inputs trigger reactive updates — the chart, summary stats, and interpretation all refresh instantly with no button press needed.
:::
:::

::: {}
**Live Output (Main Panel)**

```
Trend Over Time
│
│      ·
│    ·   ·
│  ·       ·  ·
│·              ·
└──────────────────
  2000         2020
```

::: {.card style="margin-top:0.6em;"}
📋 **Summary Stats**  
Mean: 78.4 · Min: 77.1 · Max: 79.6
:::

::: {.card}
🧠 **Auto-Interpretation**  
"From 2000 to 2020, life expectancy shows an increasing trend. The average value is 78.4…"
:::

:::

:::

::: notes
**[Speaker 2 — ~60 sec]**

Here's the app. *[Walk through the live app if presenting in person, or describe the mockup.]*

On the left sidebar, the user picks a country — say, India — an indicator — life expectancy — and drags the year slider to 1990 through 2022.

The moment you release the slider, three things update simultaneously. The line chart redraws. The summary stats table refreshes — you see the mean, min, and max instantly. And the interpretation block generates a plain-English sentence describing the trend direction.

Now watch what happens when I switch to infant mortality for Brazil. *[switch]* The chart flips, the stats recalculate, and the interpretation updates — all within about one second, because the World Bank API is fast and our reactive pipeline has no unnecessary rerenders.

There's no submit button. No loading spinner you're staring at for ten seconds. It just works.
:::

---

## Under the Hood: The Pipeline {background-color="#F0F4F8"}

::: {style="display:grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(9, 1fr); gap:0.3em; align-items:center; margin: 1em 0;"}

<div class="pipeline-step">1<br><small>Input</small></div>
<div class="arrow">→</div>
<div class="pipeline-step">2<br><small>Retrieve</small></div>
<div class="arrow">→</div>
<div class="pipeline-step">3<br><small>Clean</small></div>
<div class="arrow">→</div>
<div class="pipeline-step">4<br><small>Analyze</small></div>
<div class="arrow">→</div>
<div class="pipeline-step">5<br><small>Interpret</small></div>

:::

<br>

::: {.grid-2}

::: {}
**API Call** <span class="code-label">get_wb_data()</span>

```r
url <- paste0(
  "https://api.worldbank.org/v2/country/",
  country, "/indicator/", indicator,
  "?format=json&date=", start_year, ":", end_year
)
res  <- GET(url)
data <- fromJSON(content(res, "text"))
```

No API key required — fully public.
:::

::: {}
**Reactive Chain** <span class="code-label">server.R</span>

```r
data_reactive <- reactive({
  get_wb_data(
    input$country,
    input$indicator,
    input$years[1], input$years[2]
  )
})
# All outputs share this one reactive —
# no duplicate API calls.
```
:::

:::

::: notes
**[Speaker 1 — ~50 sec]**

Five stages. The user's input flows into an API call — we build the URL dynamically and call the World Bank's REST endpoint. No API key, no authentication, fully public.

The JSON comes back, we extract the second element of the list — that's where the World Bank puts the actual data rows — convert the date and value columns to numeric, filter out NAs, and sort by year.

One important design choice: all three outputs — the chart, the stats, and the interpretation — share a **single reactive expression**. That means the API is only called once per user interaction, not three times. Shiny caches the result and fans it out to all three render functions.

The interpretation is purely rule-based: we compare the first and last value to determine trend direction, then format a sentence. Simple, but it works without any external AI dependency.
:::

---

## Design Decisions & Trade-offs {background-color="#F0F4F8"}

::: {.grid-2}

::: {}
### ✅ What We Chose

::: {.card .tradeoff-pro}
**Live API over static file**  
Always current data; no manual refresh cycle needed
:::

::: {.card .tradeoff-pro}
**Single reactive expression**  
One API call per interaction — faster, avoids rate limits
:::

::: {.card .tradeoff-pro}
**Rule-based interpretation**  
No external LLM dependency; fully reproducible output
:::

::: {.card .tradeoff-pro}
**Minimal UI surface**  
Three controls only — low cognitive load, fast to learn
:::
:::

::: {}
### ⚠️ The Trade-offs

::: {.card .tradeoff-con}
**Live API = latency risk**  
If World Bank API is slow or down, app stalls — no offline fallback
:::

::: {.card .tradeoff-con}
**No multi-country comparison**  
Can't overlay India vs Brazil on one chart — single series only
:::

::: {.card .tradeoff-con}
**Rule-based interpretation is shallow**  
Misses seasonality, outliers, policy events — just trend direction
:::

::: {.card .tradeoff-con}
**4 countries, 3 indicators hardcoded**  
Scope was intentionally limited; real use needs a broader picker
:::
:::

:::

::: notes
**[Speaker 2 — ~50 sec]**

Every design decision is a trade-off.

We chose live API calls over bundling a static dataset. The upside is you always get current data. The downside is latency — if the World Bank's servers are slow, the user waits. A production version would cache responses server-side.

We chose a single shared reactive for all three outputs. This keeps the app snappy and avoids hammering the API. The trade-off is that it couples the outputs — if you want the chart to update on different triggers than the stats panel, you'd need to split them.

The rule-based interpretation is deliberate. It makes the output fully deterministic and reproducible — the same inputs always produce the same sentence. An LLM would be richer but would add cost, latency, and non-determinism. For a research tool, we preferred predictability.

The biggest limitation is the hardcoded scope — four countries, three indicators. That was right for a prototype but limits real-world utility.
:::

---

## What We'd Improve {background-color="#0D1B2A"}

::: {style="color:white;"}

::: {.grid-2}

::: {}
### Next Sprint

::: {style="color:#CBD5E1; font-size:0.9em;"}
🌍 &nbsp; **Expand country coverage** — full World Bank country list via a searchable dropdown

📈 &nbsp; **Multi-country overlay** — compare up to 3 countries on a single chart

💾 &nbsp; **Response caching** — cache API responses for 24h to reduce latency and handle outages

📥 &nbsp; **Export button** — download the current chart as PNG or the data as CSV
:::
:::

::: {}
### Longer Term

::: {style="color:#CBD5E1; font-size:0.9em;"}
🧠 &nbsp; **Smarter interpretation** — detect outliers, policy-change inflections, and plateau periods

🗺 &nbsp; **Choropleth map view** — visualize a single indicator globally by year

🔗 &nbsp; **Multi-indicator correlation** — scatter plot mode: does GDP growth predict life expectancy?

🐳 &nbsp; **Dockerized deployment** — one-command reproducible environment for sharing
:::
:::

:::

:::

::: notes
**[Speaker 1 — ~40 sec]**

Given more time, the most impactful immediate upgrade would be caching. Right now every slider drag hits the API. A simple 24-hour server-side cache — even just `memoise` in R — would make the app feel instant for common queries and survive API outages.

Multi-country overlay is the most-requested feature in similar tools. Letting users put India, Brazil, and the US on one chart changes the analytical value dramatically.

Longer term, the most interesting direction is the smarter interpretation layer — moving beyond "increasing or decreasing" to detecting outlier years, inflection points tied to policy changes, and convergence patterns across countries.

And Docker would make the reproducibility story airtight — right now you need R installed locally; a container removes that dependency entirely.
:::

---

## {background-color="#0D1B2A"}

::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; justify-content:center; align-items:center; height:85vh; text-align:center;"}

<p style="color:#5EEAD4; font-size:0.9em; letter-spacing:0.1em; text-transform:uppercase;">Thank You</p>

<h2 style="color:white; font-size:1.8em; margin: 0.3em 0 0.5em 0;">
  Live. Interactive. Reproducible.
</h2>

<p style="color:#CBD5E1; max-width:600px; font-size:0.95em; line-height:1.6;">
  The Global Health Trend Explorer turns a three-click selection into an instant,  
  reproducible analysis — no downloads, no API keys, no manual steps.
</p>

<br>

::: {style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; gap:1em; max-width:550px;"}
<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">0</div><div class="lbl">API keys needed</div></div>
<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">&lt;2s</div><div class="lbl">Avg response time</div></div>
<div class="stat-box"><div class="num">∞</div><div class="lbl">Reproducible</div></div>
:::

<br>
<p style="color:#64748B; font-size:0.8em;">Questions?</p>

:::

::: notes
**[Speaker 2 — ~20 sec]**

To summarize: we set out to make global health data explorable, live, and reproducible without any friction. We think the app delivers that. The trade-offs were intentional — simplicity and reliability over breadth.

We have about a minute for questions — happy to go deeper on the reactive architecture, the interpretation logic, or the roadmap.
:::
